draft:
A 15-minute AI exercise for any team:

Take one annoying weekly task. Write the current steps. Circle the steps that require judgment. Let AI touch only the uncircled steps first.

The goal is not autonomy.

The goal is a workflow people can trust twice.

draft: The split I care about: can the team inspect the workflow? Open weights help, but so do traces: context used, tool run, change made, failure seen, and where a human can stop it. Access without reviewability still feels closed.
notes: Retried at 2026-06-30T20:35+05:30. Target was still active at 8,917 views, 26 likes, 6 replies, 3 bookmarks. Inline composer accepted text and Reply was clicked, but it did not verify on with_replies. Move to manual packet.

draft: The useful version of "AI team" is less about parallel agents and more about clean interfaces between them. I would define one owner agent per step, the handoff artifact it must produce, the evidence it needs to show, and the stop condition for human review. Otherwise multiple agents just multiply ambiguity faster.
notes: Retried at 2026-06-30T20:40+05:30. Target was still active at 74 reactions and 51 comments. Editor opened but rejected typed text from fresh state. Move to manual packet.

draft: The connector point is underrated. Apps that expose clean intents, permissions, state, and reversible actions will feel native inside assistants. The edge is not an AI feature; it is making the product legible enough for agents.

draft: Benchmarks matter less once models cross good enough. For operators, the question becomes: can it plan, call tools, recover from bad context, and leave a trace a human can review? That is where agentic models start to separate.

draft: The interesting part is not just access returning. It is what teams do with a stronger model once the constraint disappears: same workflows, or better review loops? AI adoption gets stuck when capability improves faster than process.
